text: What, to the American slave, is your 4th of July? I answer: a day that reveals to him, more than all other days in the year, the gross injustice and cruelty to which he is the constant victim.
source: What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?
speech: What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?
context: Delivered to the Rochester Ladies' Anti-Slavery Society; a searing indictment of American hypocrisy.

text: The manhood of the slave is conceded. It is admitted in the fact that Southern statute books are covered with enactments forbidding the teaching of the slave to read or to write.
source: What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?
speech: What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?
context: Pointing out that slave codes themselves prove the humanity they try to deny.

text: I have often been asked to explain the manner in which I escaped from slavery. I have never approved of the very public manner in which some of our western friends have conducted what they call the Underground Railroad.
source: My Bondage and My Freedom (autobiographical lectures)
context: Refusing to endanger the secret routes while still telling his story powerfully.

text: Power concedes nothing without a demand. It never did and it never will. Find out just what any people will quietly submit to and you have found out the exact measure of injustice and wrong which will be imposed upon them.
source: West India Emancipation speech
context: On the necessity of agitation and resistance for any real progress.

text: The white man's happiness cannot be purchased by the black man's misery.
source: What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?
speech: What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?
context: Rejecting any notion that American prosperity could justify slavery.

text: If there is no struggle, there is no progress. Those who profess to favor freedom, and yet depreciate agitation, are men who want crops without plowing up the ground.
source: West India Emancipation speech
context: One of Douglass's most famous and enduring statements on reform.

text: I would unite with anybody to do right and with nobody to do wrong.
source: Speech at the American Anti-Slavery Society
context: Expressing pragmatic willingness to work across lines for the cause.

text: The limits of tyrants are prescribed by the endurance of those whom they oppress.
source: West India Emancipation speech
context: Emphasizing that oppression lasts only as long as the oppressed allow it.

text: I am a Republican, a black, dyed-in-the-wool Republican, and I never intend to belong to any other party than the party of freedom and progress.
source: Speech on behalf of Republican candidates
context: Affirming political allegiance after emancipation and during Reconstruction.

text: A man who will not labor to gain his rights is a man who would not, if he had them, prize and defend them.
source: West India Emancipation speech
context: Linking struggle to the value placed on liberty.

text: The American people have always been anxious to know what they shall do with the Negro. I have had but one answer from the beginning. Do nothing with us!
source: What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?
speech: What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?
context: Demanding simple justice and equal rights rather than paternalistic schemes.

text: I prayed for freedom for twenty years, but received no answer until I prayed with my legs.
source: Autobiographical reflection (often delivered in lectures)
context: Highlighting the necessity of action alongside prayer.

text: The soul that is within me no man can degrade.
source: Speech at the American and Foreign Anti-Slavery Society
context: Affirming the indestructible dignity of the enslaved person.

text: I expose slavery in this country, because to expose it is to kill it. Slavery is one of those monsters of darkness to whom the light of truth is death.
source: What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?
speech: What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?
context: Justifying the strategy of moral agitation.

text: No man can put a chain about the ankle of his fellow man without at last finding the other end fastened about his own neck.
source: Speech in England / various American addresses
context: The moral and spiritual cost of oppression to the oppressor.

text: I have no love for America, as such; I have no patriotism. I have no country. What country have I? The institutions of this country do not know me, do not recognize me as a man.
source: What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?
speech: What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?
context: Expressing the alienation of the enslaved and free Black Americans.

text: The arm of the Lord is not shortened, and the doom of slavery is certain.
source: West India Emancipation speech
context: Expressing ultimate confidence in the triumph of justice.

text: I would rather be a free man in my grave than a slave beneath the sod.
source: Speech at the American Anti-Slavery Society
context: Declaring that death was preferable to living in bondage.

text: The simplest truths often meet the sternest resistance and are slowest in getting general acceptance.
source: West India Emancipation speech
context: On the difficulty of persuading people to give up comfortable falsehoods.

text: Once you learn to read, you will be forever free.
source: Frequently attributed and delivered in lectures on education and liberation
context: Core belief in education as the path out of mental and physical slavery.
